skill.name: python-design-patterns
skill.description: Python design patterns including KISS, Separation of Concerns, Single Responsibility, and composition over inheritance. Use when making architecture decisions, refactoring code structure, or evaluating when abstractions are appropriate.
skill.summary: Apply practical Python design principles for simpler, testable, and maintainable code.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown code fences, example SQL strings, and example email notification calls. AI review found these are documentation-only Python examples in SKILL.md, with no executable scripts, network calls, prompt injection, or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Finding: Markdown Code Fences
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The external command alerts are Markdown code fences and Python examples, not Ruby backticks or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged regions are fenced documentation examples in a Markdown skill file. No executable script, command runner, subprocess call, or shell command is present.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Finding: Example Notification Sender
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The network alerts are illustrative email notification calls inside a composition example, not an implemented email-sending capability.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines instantiate and call a placeholder SMTP client inside example code. The skill contains no real SMTP configuration, network endpoint, credential use, or runnable implementation.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Finding: Weak Cryptography Keywords
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak cryptography alerts match ordinary prose and headings about Python design patterns, not cryptographic algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain metadata, the title, and usage bullets. No DES, MD5, SHA1, cipher configuration, hashing code, or security-sensitive crypto context appears there.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Finding: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reconnaissance alerts are SQL SELECT examples and class construction examples used to explain repository boundaries.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are static examples for data access and object composition. They do not inspect the host system, enumerate files, collect environment data, or execute commands.

content.value_statement: Python projects often accumulate tangled classes, mixed responsibilities, and premature abstractions. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code toward simpler structure, clearer boundaries, and testable components.
actual_capabilities: Explains KISS, single responsibility, separation of concerns, and composition over inheritance.
Gives Python examples for handlers, services, repositories, notifications, and dependency injection.
Helps evaluate whether an abstraction is justified by repeated real use cases.
Suggests boundaries between API, business logic, data access, and external integrations.
Identifies common maintainability problems such as leaked ORM models and mixed I/O logic.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a runnable refactoring tool.
It does not analyze a repository unless the user supplies code or context.
It focuses on general Python structure, not framework-specific architecture rules.
It does not replace performance, security, or database reviews.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Service Boundary
use_cases.description: Use the skill to separate handlers, services, repositories, and integration code before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Refactor Tangled Python Code
use_cases.description: Use the skill to identify mixed responsibilities and propose smaller, testable functions or classes.

use_cases.title: Review Abstraction Choices
use_cases.description: Use the skill to decide when duplication is acceptable and when a shared abstraction is justified.

prompt_templates.title: Check Basic Simplicity
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small module
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Python module using KISS and single responsibility. Point out unnecessary complexity and suggest simpler alternatives.

prompt_templates.title: Separate Responsibilities
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this Python code for mixed HTTP, validation, business, and data access concerns. Propose a clearer structure with focused responsibilities.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Composition or Inheritance
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether this design should use inheritance or composition. Explain the tradeoffs and propose a testable Python structure.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Architecture Decisions
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Python architecture for premature abstractions, coupling, layer violations, and dependency injection problems. Provide prioritized recommendations.

output_examples.input: A request handler validates input, writes SQL, sends email, and formats the response.
output_examples.output: The handler has too many reasons to change. Move validation into input models, business rules into a service, and persistence into a repository.

output_examples.input: Two similar functions process different workflows with different validation rules.
output_examples.output: Keep the duplication for now. The workflows are not proven to share the same abstraction, so apply the rule of three.

output_examples.input: A notification class subclasses a base service and creates its own SMTP client.
output_examples.output: Prefer composition. Inject an email sender so tests can use a fake sender and production can use a real adapter.

best_practices: Start with the simplest structure that meets current requirements.
Keep I/O adapters separate from business rules where practical.
Use dependency injection for services that need databases, caches, clients, or loggers.
anti_patterns: Creating factories, registries, or abstract bases before repeated use proves a need.
Returning ORM models directly from API handlers.
Mixing SQL queries, external calls, and domain decisions in one function.

faq.question: Does this skill generate full application architectures?
faq.answer: It can propose structure, but it works best when grounded in your requirements and existing code.

faq.question: Can it refactor code automatically?
faq.answer: It provides guidance and examples. Codex or Claude Code can apply edits when you give repository access.

faq.question: Is this only for large projects?
faq.answer: No. The guidance also helps small projects avoid complexity and keep modules easy to test.

faq.question: Does it recommend every classic design pattern?
faq.answer: No. It favors practical Python principles and warns against premature pattern use.

faq.question: Can it help with testing?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how smaller responsibilities and injected dependencies make isolated tests easier.

faq.question: Does it cover security architecture?
faq.answer: Only indirectly. It is focused on maintainability, so security-sensitive designs still need security review.
